name: Grooved Spheres
description: The Grooved Spheres of South Africa are a collection of metallic spheres, primarily found near Ottosdal, South Africa, that have sparked considerable debate. Proponents of alternative history and ancient technology theories suggest these spheres are evidence of advanced, prehistoric civilizations, potentially dating back millions of years. The spheres, typically iron pyrite or hematite, exhibit natural or artificial grooves and ridges. Some are even claimed to contain a soft, spongy material on the inside. Alternative theories posit that the spheres were crafted by intelligent beings for unknown purposes, possibly as tools, energy sources, or even navigational devices. Mainstream geology, however, attributes the spheres to natural geological processes. Specifically, they are considered concretions - masses of sedimentary rock formed through the precipitation of minerals around a central nucleus. The grooves are explained as Liesegang banding, a natural phenomenon that occurs during the formation of concretions in layered sedimentary rocks. While some spheres may appear unusually shaped or patterned, geologists argue that natural variations in mineral composition, pressure, and other environmental factors during the concretion process can account for these features. The age of the host rock formations, approximately 2.8 to 3 billion years old, is often cited by proponents as evidence of their extraordinary age, but geologists emphasize that the age of the rock does not necessarily correlate with the age of the concretions themselves. Concretions can form at any time after the sediment is deposited and lithified. The debate continues, with proponents emphasizing the unusual shapes and metallic composition, while mainstream science attributes them to well-understood geological processes.
subtitle: Ancient artifacts or natural concretions?

claims.claim: The spheres are too perfectly shaped to be natural.
claims.evidence: Some spheres exhibit near-perfect sphericity and parallel grooves.
claims.criticism: Natural processes, like Liesegang banding, can produce symmetrical patterns and rounded shapes in concretions. Imperfections are often present upon closer inspection.

claims.claim: The spheres are made of an unusual, artificially created metal alloy.
claims.evidence: Analyses have shown the presence of iron pyrite and hematite with trace elements.
claims.criticism: Iron pyrite and hematite are common minerals found in concretions. The trace elements are consistent with the geological environment.

claims.claim: The age of the spheres proves the existence of advanced prehistoric civilizations.
claims.evidence: The spheres are found in Precambrian rock formations dated to billions of years ago.
claims.criticism: While the host rock is ancient, the concretions themselves could have formed much later within that rock. Concretion formation is an ongoing process.

keyFigures.name: Roelf Marx
keyFigures.role: Curator of the Museum of Klerksdorp, who publicized the spheres

mythologyConnections: None directly, but linked to broader themes of lost civilizations and advanced ancient knowledge.
Possible connections to creation myths involving spherical objects in other cultures.

timeline.event: Spheres discovered near Ottosdal, South Africa.
Theories about their origin and purpose emerge, debated by proponents and geologists.

sources.author: Cremo, Michael A. & Thompson, Richard L.
sources.work: Forbidden Archeology: The Hidden History of the Human Race
sources.title: Cremo, Michael A. & Thompson, Richard L.

sources.author: McIver, Alex
sources.work: Geological Excursion Guide Book, No. 1, Witwatersrand Gold Field
sources.title: McIver, Alex
